headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no known family receives full military honors after community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting 'insane' government benefit scams after billions stolen
headlines.domestic: 87% of voters expect AI to transform lives, but only 47% find it useful today

metadata.daily_do: Check if there's an 'unclaimed' veteran burial happening in your area and consider attending

clips.caption: When Duke gets emotional about veteran burials
Pringle explains how Romans honored their dead warriors
Jax discovers AI is being used to find office chairs

script.text: Every time I see one of these stories, I think about the guys who didn't make it back with me. And I wonder if anybody's gonna show up for them.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th, and we're starting with something that'll remind you why community still matters.

Geopolitics first. In Nashville, a Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman was scheduled to be buried alone last Tuesday. He had no known family, no one to claim his remains. But word got out, and hundreds of people showed up.
Oh wow.

Veterans, community members, complete strangers. They filled the sanctuary, stood outside the church, and gave him full military honors. The VA rep said quote, 'When the paperwork came across my desk marked unclaimed veteran, I say that's incorrect. Thanks to this support, we are able to claim our honorable veterans.'
That's— that's what it should be.

Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced the DOJ is launching an entirely new fraud division to combat what he calls the 'insane' problem of government benefit fraud. This comes after billions in taxpayer funds were stolen, including a four million dollar scam where two Pennsylvania men created fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis.
Four million for fake homeless shelters? That's like, evil startup energy.
Blanche says they're also finding daycare centers that quote 'do not exist' receiving millions in federal lunch money. The new division will have its own assistant attorney general.
I mean, good? This stuff is why people don't trust government programs.

And domestic. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, with fifty-three percent predicting major transformation. But here's the disconnect — only forty-seven percent find AI useful in their daily lives right now.
Ah, the classic gap between expectation and experience.
Eighty percent are concerned AI is eroding trust in news and social media. And in a delightful sidebar, seventy-four percent of Americans believe life exists or existed on other planets.
Wait, more people believe in aliens than find AI useful? That tracks, honestly.
Alright, let's dig into this veteran story because, um, it's hitting me harder than I expected. Duke, you served. What goes through your mind when you hear about Lonnie Wayman?
Look, every time I see one of these stories, I think about the guys who didn't make it back with me. And I wonder if anybody's gonna show up for them.
The thing is, military service creates this bond. Even if you never met the guy, you know he raised his right hand and said I'll serve. That means something.

But the fact that this is even necessary points to a bigger systemic issue, right? We have twenty-two veteran suicides a day, massive homelessness rates among vets, and now we're finding out some are literally dying alone.
Bree, I agree with the problem. But this story isn't about the VA failing. It's about community stepping up when government can't reach everyone.
I'm not— look, I'm glad people showed up. That's beautiful. But we shouldn't need to crowdsource basic dignity for veterans.
Can I ask something? How does a veteran just... disappear? Like, the military keeps track of everything. They know what socks I wore to basic training, probably.
It happens more than you think. Guy gets out, moves around, maybe struggles with PTSD or addiction, loses touch with family. Twenty years later, he's just... gone.
The VA has programs to find isolated veterans, but they're not perfect. According to their own data, they estimate there are thousands of veterans who've, quote, 'fallen through the cracks.'
Right, so this is where I think we need better outreach, better mental health services, better housing support. The community response is wonderful, but it's treating the symptom, not the disease.
But here's where you lose me. You want to expand government programs to solve a problem that government programs helped create?
How did government programs create veterans dying alone?
Because we sent them to war, brought them back broken, and then handed them over to a bureaucracy that treats them like case numbers instead of people.
Okay, but that's an argument for better programs, not no programs.
Or it's an argument for communities doing what they do best — taking care of their own. What happened in Nashville was perfect. No bureaucrat made that happen. People just showed up.
I think you're both right. The community response was beautiful and necessary. But Duke, not every community has the capacity or knowledge to identify isolated veterans.
Also, can we talk about how this went viral? Like, somebody had to put this on social media for hundreds of people to show up. What if nobody had posted about it?
Exactly! We can't rely on random social media posts to determine which veterans get honored and which ones don't.
Fair enough. But I still think the lesson here is about what communities can do when they care. Not what Washington should do when they don't.
The ceremony ended with doves being released near the flagpoles. Lonnie Wayman was buried with full military honors, surrounded by people who never met him but honored his service anyway.
That's beautiful. I'm not crying, you're crying.
The doves thing got me too. Like, someone thought to bring doves. To a stranger's funeral. That's next-level humanity right there.
Professor, this story has to connect to something in history. Communities honoring unknown soldiers?
Oh my, yes. This is as old as warfare itself. But let me tell you about Rome, because they understood something we've forgotten.

When a Roman legionnaire died, even in some distant frontier, the army would perform the ritual of calling his name three times. If no one answered, he was considered officially dead. But here's the crucial part — every soldier's funeral was the responsibility of his unit, his brothers.
They had better veteran services than we do.
In a way, yes! But more than that, they understood that military service creates what the Romans called 'sacramentum' — a sacred bond. Not just to the emperor, but to each other.

Now, fast forward to 1921. America creates the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. We take one unidentified soldier from the Great War and say, 'This represents all of them.' The whole country honors this one man because he represents every man who served.
So Nashville was like a micro version of that?

Precisely! Lonnie Wayman became everyone's unknown soldier. The community said, 'If you served, you're ours to honor.' It's the same impulse that built Arlington National Cemetery, the same impulse that makes the British guard the Cenotaph.
Doc, that's exactly right. When I see that folded flag, I don't think about politics or budgets. I think about the promise we made to each other.

And here's what's remarkable — this tradition transcends every political system. Soviet Russia had their eternal flame. Communist China honors their People's Liberation Army dead. Even societies that disagree on everything else agree that warriors deserve honor.

Because societies understand, even if they can't articulate it, that military service is the ultimate social contract. 'I will risk my life for people I've never met.' That deserves recognition from people who never met you.
So Nashville was just doing what humans have always done.
Exactly. The forms change — Roman coins on the eyes, Egyptian burial goods, American flag folding ceremonies. But the impulse is identical. Honor those who served. Don't let them go unmarked.
Man, I was not ready for history class to make me cry today.

Alright, I need to emotionally recover, so let's talk about some absolutely unhinged stuff happening in America. First up: Palantir, the company that makes AI for the military, is now using their artificial intelligence... to find office chairs for government employees.
I'm sorry, what?

I kid you not. The same AI that tracks terrorists is now optimizing ergonomic seating solutions. Somewhere, there's an algorithm that knows both where Bin Laden's nephew is hiding and which Herman Miller chair has the best lumbar support.
This is why people don't trust government spending.
Wait, but actually, if they can make government procurement more efficient—
Bree. They're using military-grade AI to buy furniture.
Okay, yeah, that's insane.
Meanwhile, in Ireland, a workplace harassment case that's gonna make your day. A boss repeatedly shouted the word 'potato' at his Irish employee. Just... potato. Over and over.
Good heavens.
The employee won the case, obviously. The judge basically said, 'Sir, you cannot ethnically harass someone with root vegetables.' Which is a sentence that shouldn't need to exist, but here we are.
I have so many questions, but I don't want answers to any of them.

Smart choice. Speaking of questionable choices, Louisiana classrooms are required to post the Ten Commandments by this Friday. Which seems like a tight deadline for something that's definitely gonna end up in court.
Oh, that's absolutely going to the Supreme Court.
Yeah, but imagine being the teacher trying to explain to kids why 'Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife' is hanging next to the multiplication tables.
I feel like there are pedagogical challenges there.

And finally, in Florida — because of course it's Florida — a drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. Months, people. The guy was living in a ten-by-ten storage unit like he was furniture.
How is that not kidnapping?
Oh, it totally is! The dealer got arrested. But imagine being the guy who finds him. You're looking for your Christmas decorations and instead you find Gary from accounting who owes someone money.
This country never ceases to amaze me.
Professor, in all of history, has anyone ever been imprisoned in a storage unit?
Not that I can recall, but the Romans did have some creative punishment methods. This feels distinctly modern.
The storage unit economy has really gotten out of hand.
Right? We've got people living in storage units, AI buying office chairs, and bosses screaming about potatoes. This is why aliens won't visit us.
Speaking of aliens, remember seventy-four percent of Americans think they exist somewhere out there.
After today's stories, I don't blame them for staying away.

Alright, time for the Daily Do. Today's is simple and important. Check if there's an 'unclaimed' veteran burial happening in your area. Most states have programs where they notify the public when a veteran with no family is being laid to rest.

You can usually find these through your state veterans' affairs office, local VFW posts, or sometimes funeral homes post about them on social media. It takes an hour of your time, and it means everything to someone who served.
Leo, that's perfect. And if you can't make it to a service, a lot of these programs need volunteers to help identify isolated veterans in the first place.
There's also the Veterans Community Living Centers in most states that always need visitors. Just saying.
Professor, any final thoughts?
Lincoln said it best: 'To care for him who shall have borne the battle and for his widow and his orphan.' That includes caring for him in death. Nashville understood that.
And on that note, this has been The Friction. I'm Jax, reminding you that while AI might find your office chair, only humans can find your humanity. We'll see you tomorrow.
Thanks for listening, everyone. Take care of each other out there.
